_meta.what_firm_does: Twilio is a developer-first cloud communications platform (APIs for SMS, voice, email, etc.). This L2 PM role works on the unified Console platform: intelligent/AI-powered console capabilities, natural-language interactions, M&A integration (e.g., Segment), legacy deprecation, developer experience, partnering with Eng/Design/Edge teams.
_meta.jd_wants: 1-2 yrs PM/entrepreneurial/intensive-project experience (recent grads w/ startup/multidisciplinary experience welcome); understanding of developer platforms, APIs, headless architectures; tactical execution balancing reliability + delivery; strategic problem-solving; behavioral analytics; cross-channel DX design.
_meta.status: DRAFT — STRETCH: developer-platform/API PM role. Years bar is low (1-2 yrs, recent grads OK) so Jamie clears that, but the technical developer-platform context is a genuine gap. Answers are HONEST about it and lean on PM-transferable strengths + her real AI/RAG project. Jamie to decide whether to apply.

Why are you interested in this role?: What pulls me to this role is that the most formative lesson of my career is a product lesson. At InGenius Prep I shipped a new process with a full document library and assumed adoption would follow—it didn't. I interviewed the users, found the real blocker was friction not resistance, and shipped a one-pager that fixed it. That taught me to do discovery before I build and to trust behavioral data over assumptions, which is the heart of product management. An L2 PM role lets me do that work full-time, and Twilio's push to turn the Console into an intelligent, AI-powered environment is exactly the kind of problem I want to work on.
Why do you want to work at Twilio?: Two reasons. First, the AI direction genuinely excites me—I've been building a RAG-based system in my own time that reasons over a large body of documents, so making a tool into an intelligent, natural-language operating environment is something I'm hands-on with, not just reading about. Second, the role fits how I work: I diagnose the real user problem, prioritize with data, and execute cross-functionally. I'll be honest that I haven't been a developer-platform PM and would be ramping on APIs and headless architectures, but I'm a fast study and the PM fundamentals transfer directly.
How do you prioritize what to build?: I prioritize by impact, grounded in data and user discovery rather than the loudest request. When I led a self-started initiative at NextGen, I analyzed 2,000+ data points specifically to find which lever moved engagement most before recommending anything—I didn't want to fix a symptom. In product terms, I'd look at behavioral signals to find where users actually hit friction, weigh that against reliability and delivery trade-offs, and sequence the work so we ship something real and measurable. Then I'd track whether it moved the metric and iterate. Diagnose, prioritize by evidence, ship, measure, repeat.
Tell us about yourself / anything else: I'm an analytical organizer with a growth mindset, grounded in an MS in Applied Organizational Psychology from USC, where research design and measurement were core. In practice I diagnose with data, build with users not just for them, collaborate cross-functionally, and track the metrics that prove impact. I'm fluent with AI tools and actively building an AI product on the side. I'm candid that developer-platform depth is something I'd grow into, and confident in the product instincts—discovery, prioritization, execution, and comfort with ambiguity—I'd bring on day one.
